comment_text:
When I was a teenager. (Back in the 70's & 80's). I had them so bad across my cheeks. 
My parents would tease me, put me down, blame me for my "ugliness". How many times
I heard how cute my face was, and how pretty I'd be with those green eyes and long blonde hair and pretty little figure. If I didn't have all those zits all over my face.
Imagine being in a group of people, having a conversation about rock music or college. Always someone randomly blurts out "zit advice". Then all heads and eyes turn to you. Eyes studying your face for every imperfection. This actually went on for two decades. To this day (I no longer have acne etc) I still have issues with looking people in the face. Putting my head down. I always wore long bangs and long hair to cover my face as much as possible. I never wore makeup due to some of the most obnoxious comments made. Especially back then when a lot of falsities about acne etc. Were considered facts. Like you must be dirty. You don't wash your face. You did something wrong. Especially being at those teen early twenties years. It's a lot of guilt to carry. 
Even my sister would say things when a boy would say I was pretty, built or a natural blonde, etc. She'd say, but she's got a zit face up close. Then I was allergic to Salicylic acid. I tried so many treatments over the years. Unfortunately for me the only thing that worked getting rid of those bastards was menopause.

comment_text: For the love of God, don't just randomly start using salicylic acid and/or retinol everyday! Both can be very irritating alone and especially together. Salicylic acid is oil soluble and very effective at treating blackheads. Start using it only once or twice a week and gradually increase if there are no signs of irritation (it can take days for the effects to irritate skin). Once you get on a good regimen with the SA then you can add retinol. Same rules apply,  once or twice a week then gradually increase. The difference with retinol, though, is that it causes photosensitivity and should only be used at nighttime, and you should wear spf all day every day. There is also a natural alternative to retinol that is as effective, if not more, called bakuchiol (ba•koo•chee•ul) that is much less irritating and does not cause photosensitivity and therefore can be used during the day.

comment_text: When my younger brother was around four years old (he's now 72) he developed a nose bleed that wouldn't stop completely and after a couple of days his nose started to turn red on the affected side and he started really complaining. Our mom couldn't get hold of whatever was in the nostril and it was apparent that there WAS a blockage but impossible to tell what it was so we took him to the family doctor and after calming him down and giving him some liquid that REALLY relaxed him he went up in the darkness and finally was able to pull out the foreign body, after five minutes or so to get it cleaned off enough to identify the offending object it turned out to be one of the real old treadle type Singer sewing machine bobbins. Those old ones have no resemblance at all to the newer ('60's and later) ones they are about an inch and a half long and a little over a quarter of an inch in diameter and have to be dropped into a bullet shaped housing in the machine and is usually wound completely flush with the outside diameter with thread.

comment_text: I see a lot of people asking why he didn't just cut it off.  She explains this in the description.   An example is my grandfather.  He had one on his nose.  They cut it off.  It grew back bigger.   They cut that one off.  He grew two more.  They grew huge and they got cut off.  At 93 he had 17 of these on his face alone and the three on his nose were roughly the size of a marble, a golf ball and a tennis ball.  On his death at age 93 you could not tell he was a human being by looking at his face.  He could not see out of his left eye and he had difficulty breathing and eating.   All because his doctor kept insisting that the only treatment was to cut them off.   I wish we had taken some photos as a warning to others.  But as you can imagine, he was camera shy at the end.

comment_text: I breast fed all four of my babies. After my second daughter had turned 6 weeks old, I got one of those cysts. OMG. It was about 2 inches deep and 2.5 to 3 inches wide. I was in so much pain. I had gotten a plugged milk gland that caused the cyst. The doctor that treated it didn't give me anything for pain. This was 1972. He swabbed it out. I thought I was going to die. He then dumped benedine (sp?) directly into what looked like to me a massive hole in the side of my breast. For two days I sobbed from the pain and had to keep dumping that disinfectant into the hole. The third day it finally started feeling better. I had to quit breast feeding my daughter as a result, but at least I did for six weeks.  I wouldn't wish that on anyone. I don't think I will ever forget that one.

comment_text: Omg this is why when I sense a bee close to my face, I cover my nose and ears as much as possible!! I REMEMBER SO VIVIDLY when I was like 7~8 ish years old. I was standing outside my house on like a patch of grass, and it was a weird, quiet day. A fricking bumblebee came and kinda hovered around me, so I was contemplating on should I shoo it away? And as I was kinda thinking to myself, oh it's getting awfully close, it DECIDED TO LAND ON MY LEFT EAR. I COULD FEEL THE LITTLE FUZZ BALL BUZZ AS IT STUCK ITS FUZZY FRICKING HEAD IN MY TINY ASIAN GIRL'S EAR PROBABLY READING MY FRICKING MIND IN THERE. My whole body froze, and I stood there holding my breath. I was praying it would leave soon, but it wouldn't leave for what felt like eternity (it probably was for like maybe a minute haha). Now, every time I hear the buzz, I get goosebumps all over, taking me back to that one oddly quiet day in front of my house standing there in a patch of grass, fearing for my tiny life.

Sun damage over years starts the process but progresses over time with environmental factors making it more pronounced.

comment_text: I thought I was the only weird one massaging my oily skin with oil because its an odd concept in skincare community lol but its nice to know someone else has been doing the same. I've been doing that "deep" cleansing massaging (for 10-15mins) with grapeseed or safflower oil, literally oil, in my PM routine for years now. Its a similar concept of double cleanser except this doesnt lather so I rinse off with warm water followed by a regular cleanser that can remove that oil without stripping my face. I used to do it with coconut oil years ago but grapeseed/safflower oils are better since they are lightweight. For me this is a non irritating way to exfoliate my skin in a way because I can feel my bumpy/congested skin feeling smooth afterwards. Sorry tl;dr. Just wanted to share my experience.

comment_text:
I'd totally forgotten this technique! I worked with a gal that did my nails like this back in the late 80's; we loved it so much that we started trying it with our face makeup too. 

Position your lace first, then *airbrush* over the top of it; you can even layer different colours. Seal it with 2-3 coats of a light, dry matte setting spray. I recommend primer first.

You can do it almost anywhere you'd put a tattoo, as long as it's in a spot where your clothing or hair won't rub it off. It looks especially amazing going down your neck, decolletage, or collarbone area. I loved the look so much that I nearly got it tattooed!

comment_text: If you (reader) want to do this on your hair and you don't have big curls to support it, you can do it the way I was taught. Create two pigtails either high or low, but on the last pass do what she did here and leave a small loop of hair in the hair tie. It should be vaguely sailor moon ish but as small a bump as you can make it. Then, split the falling hair into two. Twist one section loosely around the bump you made. If you do it too tight it will lose volume and if you do it too loose it will fall out, so it might take a couple tries. Then wrap the other section the opposite direction just slightly tighter and secure with another hair tie. Pin if required. Repeat on the other pigtail and boom, artsy space buns for anyone! I have cool little sunflower bobby pins I like to put around the base of mine. They also suit ribbons, clips and headbands very well, though if you wear a headscarf with them and have smooth textured hair I would pin your scarf, since the buns should be far enough back that they cannot support one.

comment_text:
Idk about this string cleaning hack, but when I was like 3-5 yo and got my first piercings (since it’s in our culture to get ears and nose pierced at a very young age) my mom didn’t let me wear any earrings till it healed completely, for fear of inflammation. Instead she used vey young and soft fresh Neem tree stems to stay inside the piercings till it healed. And mom used to change it out with fresh stems every few days so I didn’t have to wear the dried ones. Since neem has antibacterial properties and it genuinely worked wonders for me. I used to get really bad inflammation and infection when I wore earrings that weren’t strictly made out of gold, in my newly pierced ears, but once mom made me wear the Neem stems for a couple of months, it went away permanently. I can wear all kinds of earrings now…even imitation jewellery.

Edit: Okay wow!! I didn't realize my comment would get so much interaction. Thanks guys!!
Also, I'm so sorry for replying so late but I don't have the notifications on so I didn't see the questions you guys are asking. I have already answered some of your questions individually but I'll also put it here so that everyone can see.

1. Where to find neem twigs?
You can find potted neem plant in any shop that sells plants. Especially Indian plants. You can even buy a young neem plant and keep it in your house if you want.
2. How to use neem twigs?
You have to use fresh and young neem twigs and change it out with fresh ones every 3-4 days as it dries inside the piercing....or even every week if you live in a very humid climate. Just keep moving/twisting the twig in your piercing throughout the day to prevent it from closing.
3. Alternative to neem twigs?
If you cannot find neem twigs then you can use a clean and slightly burned straw or a fine thread/string and then soak in it neem oil/water. Then wear it like a hoop earring. Keep twisting it in your piercing throughout the day to prevent it from closing up and change it out every 3-4 days with a fresh one.
CAUTION: Just make sure that the neem oil you use isn't an essential oil but a pure neem oil. Same with the neem water...make sure it is just pure plain neem water without any added ingredients.
4. FUN FACT: Neem water can also be used to get rid of dandruffs and visible flakes. It works wonders for seborrheic dermatitis as well and can show results with only 3 uses. I use it myself. Just apply it like you would a serum on your scalp, massage it in and leave it on for anywhere from 1 hour upto overnight, depending on the severity of your condition, and then rinse off with your normal shower routine.
HOPE THIS HELPS GUYS!!😊😊

Best way to clean your piercings (I’m not an expert but I’ve gotten lots of ear piercings (but only ear!) is to use a recommended saline solution and to leave it alone! Some places may tell you to twist your piercings but the risk of bacteria on your fingers is larger than the risk of natural blood/ skincell build up. Check every couple of days (unless actively infected!) by feeling the back, rather than doing it every time you disinfect which should be 1-2 times daily for fresh piercings. comment_text:
Step 1: oil hair strand before washing, massage for 2-3 minutes
Step 2: use luke warm water, not hot water
Step 3: wet hair thoroughly before shampooing, and shampoo right after rinsing (don’t squeeze hair in between)
Step 4: massage shampoo into scalp using fingertips in a side to side motion, or a little scalp exfoliate, shampoo for 3 minutes. Dont shampoo all of your hair, only scalp! 
Step 5: rinse shampoo out with warm water for 1 minute, then squeeze hair dry to remove excess water, don’t roll hair, squeeze in a downwards motion 
Step 6: then apply conditioner to strands of the hair, not to the scalp! Massage product gently from mid length to end. And let it sit for 10 minutes, cover with a shower cap or a  clip. Rinse with warm- cold water 
Step 7: now thay hair is clean, remove excess water squeezing in downward motion. Towel dry with a micro fibre towel or old Tshirt, and gently press downwards
Step 8: follow up with a hair oil. Or leave in conditioner first and then hair oil. 
Tips: the less you wash your hair the better, healthy range between washes is 3-4 days.

_query_label: haircare routine, curly hair routine, fragrance review, body care routine, nail tutorial, mens grooming, beauty devices review
comment_text:
Hi Cristine, thanks for "getting serious" at the end of this video.  It is so important to talk about issues like this.  I am a female physics PhD student that has a huge holo stash and regularly comes to work with blinged out nails.  There are definitely people who take me less seriously because of it and it can be really tough.  I make an effort to make sure they don't prevent me from being feminine when I want to, and I hope to show everyone that you can be a BAMF scientist and a holosexual.  At a conference in March I presented my research with my nails decked out in super-bright Cherub by Superchic Lacquer partially to make a point (and partially because I love it).

I am on the fence about the efficacy of these products.  On the one hand, I totally agree with you - girls don't need girly or dumbed down science.  On the other hand, I can see how these products might be a bridge for young kids who like nail polish to get interested in science.  I think it might come down to the actual science content of the toy.  There is definitely science involved in nail polish - what chemistry makes a quick dry top coat quick dry?  Why do some colors water marble well and others not so much?  How do suspension bases work?  It might be a question of can questions like this be simplified to a level appropriate for the target age of the toy.  I think if the toy really makes an effort to teach the science of nail polish, then that's pretty cool.  However, if the toy is just like, "Look!  Science for girls! ...it's nail polish!" and it is not on a (appropriately) sophisticated level, I have a hard time getting on board.  I also feel like the feminizing of science in this way maybe implies that science is inherently masculine?  I do not like that idea one bit!

Anyway thanks again for bringing up this important issue!

Kathryn

PS Your job sounds really cool!

comment_text:
I'd totally forgotten this technique! I worked with a gal that did my nails like this back in the late 80's; we loved it so much that we started trying it with our face makeup too. 

Position your lace first, then *airbrush* over the top of it; you can even layer different colours. Seal it with 2-3 coats of a light, dry matte setting spray. I recommend primer first.

You can do it almost anywhere you'd put a tattoo, as long as it's in a spot where your clothing or hair won't rub it off. It looks especially amazing going down your neck, decolletage, or collarbone area. I loved the look so much that I nearly got it tattooed!

comment_text:
The brown spot on the ball gown is old blood. If it were made in a factory, it's highly likely the person making it pricked their finger and they sent it anyways. I've gotten a dress like that before.

The chemical smell is from where the packages are sent overseas and they're required to clean them with certain chemicals to kill any diseases so they won't travel over to your country. I have a $600 evening gown that literally smells like some sort of fishy smell because that's what the kind of chemical used to store it in smelled like.

Also, the reason for the short dresses is most likely because it was designed in Asia and most women there are very petite. They usually don't take that into account when making the clothes.
